tax: Greater Galeb Duhr is placed at species rank beneath Galeb Dur (Lithic order, Earth Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on fault-jointed granite body, retractable stub-limbs, and a low centre of mass that lets it right itself after a fall. It is separated from its sibling the Scree Tumblestone by its deliberate two-legged walk versus the Tumblestone's downhill roll; the two share the genus body-plan but never the rolling locomotion. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: coarse granite and pegmatite welded along its own old fracture planes.
ap: Greater Galeb Duhr stands 1.8–2.4 m and roughly two tonnes. Its silhouette is a hunched ovoid boulder with two short legs and two grasping fore-stubs. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: a horizontal fault-seam that opens into a mouth-like grin when it speaks in grinding tones; quartz nodules set where eyes would be, catching lamplight as twin dull glints; and lichen and old cave-dust still clinging to the upper hide, betraying long stillness. It moves with a slow, ground-shaking two-step that registers on a Wall-Warden's tremor-line at thirty paces, and shows no light of its own, only the cold mineral glint of its quartz eye-nodules. Even after dispersal it can be identified from paired flat-bottomed pressure prints a metre apart and a trail of shed gravel, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
eco: Greater Galeb Duhr persists in the granite scree-benches and old rockfall fans of Tier III, where settled boulder-fields give it both camouflage and raw mass. It does not breed; it forms where a long-still boulder-pile is saturated by earth-pressure welling up from a fault, and recurs whenever that fault flexes again after a tremor, re-waking the same body or assembling a fresh one from the same scree. It sustains its mass on the slow grind of tectonic pressure, drawing in loose stone, which it fuses to its mass to heal and grow. Range is bounded by the reach of the active fault: outside that band the body loses cohesion and slumps back into an ordinary boulder. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a boulder that has rolled uphill against the slope's logic, leaving a wrong-way drag-scar, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Greater Galeb Duhr is roused by footfalls, hammering, or any quarrying within its bench and otherwise lies utterly inert, posing as terrain. It claims a single scree-bench and the path that crosses it and answers intrusion with rising to block the path and rolling stone down on intruders. Toward grey-folk it is indifferent until quarried; survey crews report that Forgemasters who chip its bench wake it and rarely finish the survey. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
traitsText: Stable characters of Greater Galeb Duhr: a body of coarse granite and pegmatite welded along its own old fracture planes; immune to crushing, falling stone, and ordinary fire; and a defining weakness — splitting wedges driven into its fault-seam, which can cleave it. Its senses run through the medium itself: tremor-reading through the rock it touches; it is effectively blind and deaf to anything not in contact with stone. It tolerates heat, dust, and total darkness but fails at prolonged immersion, which floods its joints and locks its limbs. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the low ovoid build and the fault-seam mouth, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
abilities: Abilities of Greater Galeb Duhr are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it shapes and fuses surrounding stone to itself, healing damage; it rolls boulders down a slope as ranged attack; it senses approach through ground tremor at thirty paces; and it can sink into loose scree and vanish from sight. Each is a direct expression of tectonic pressure moving through a granite body, so it cannot, for instance, produce flame, fly, or cross deep water. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
bg: In the Throat, Greater Galeb Duhr matters because it is the commonest fatal 'rockfall' on Tier III that was in fact a defended elemental, and quarry crews need to read its wrong-way drag-scars. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as a terrain hazard to be sounded with a tremor-line before any bench is worked, and its presence reshapes routes through the granite benches, which crews now cross only after sounding for a walking boulder. Its waking is taken by the Stonesong Heralds as an omen that a sleeping fault has stirred.
g: Greater Galeb Duhr (Saxum voluble) is a boulder-bodied stone elemental that wakes from a settled scree-pile and walks on stub-limbs of fused rock. Documented from the broken-stone benches of lower-gorge Tier III, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — grey granite mass, fault-line joints, and a gravel hide — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. At rest it is indistinguishable from an ordinary 2 m boulder, which is the source of most fatal misidentifications. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a ring of fresh-fractured granite and a shallow depression where its mass stood and no carcass.
